content: First, there is no quick solution, no 'white list' in Thunderbird. This URL has info on available options: to aid you: https://blog.thunderbird.net/2024/09/thunderbird-and-spam/ additionally, you might check your email provider's options, as many have white lists and spam filters that work at the server level prior to the messages being available to Thunderbird. There are also some third-party products, such as Mailwasher, that can do wonders (that's what I use). You can also create a filter for known senders whose messages go to spam. Good luck.

creator: rednoise

content:
Please quote the message that's asking for the password, complete and verbatim, or post a screen shot.

The only time Thunderbird itself would ask for a password is if you have locked access to your Saved Passwords. That feature can be found at <Settings | Privacy & Security | Passwords | Use a Primary Password>.

If it's not that, it can only be your email provider asking for your password. You might find it in the Saved Passwords area I described above. If it's not there, and don't have it recorded somewhere, you will probably have to reset your password on your provider's web mail page.
creator: rednoise

V115.10.1 is the current May 2026 'monthly release.

I mention the earlier version (102) and where I download it from so it's clear how I was running that. The standard release version of 102 was installed. Not ESR. That's why it updated to v115.

Its entirely not clear what caused Thunderbird to then switch to the ESR channel. It first "updated" to the Aug2025 ESR release, before auto updating the latest ESR release v140.

That last detail may not be consequential to determining what caused me to be switched from standard to ESR. The problematic switch was going from 115 'standard' --> ESR automatically.

Ive connected a single email account to the desktop app and installed a single plugin, released this year and listed as compatible with v115. No other customization has been done.

content:
Please post a screen shot showing an example. You can redact any personal information. I want to see what you mean by "at the bottom of any saved emails". I read your description as saying that this spurious text appears in the body of the message, but I have trouble imagining that.

Does this happen in all folders or only some, or just one?

If the thing happened in just one folder, and appeared in the Message List and/or header, I would attribute it to a corrupt folder index. But that doesn't fit your description. If it was that, the fix would be to right-click on the folder name, choose Properties, then Repair Folder.
creator: rednoise

content:
Thank you for reporting this.

I think the existing account in the "Set up your existing email address" means that you should already have a working email address to add a new account in Thunderbird.

Thunderbird will not create an new email address for you, it just uses your existing email to setup a so-called account in its user interface so that you can use it.

Do you agree?

content:
Per poter inquadrare il problema prova a fornire queste informazioni:

  1. Versione di Thunderbird
  2. Versione del sistema operativo
  3. Provider delle caselle email (Gmail, Libero, Hotmail, etc.)
  4. Tipologia di account (POP3, IMAP)
  5. Estensioni installate
  6. Messaggi di errore che vengono visualizzati durante l'utilizzo di Thunderbird
  7. Software di sicurezza eventualmente presenti (Antivirus e simili)
  8. Screenshot del problema (se utile a chiarire il contesto)
